PROCEDURE: ORIF Orbital Floor 

 

Findings: [Right/left] orbital floor fracture with orbital contents herniating into the maxillary sinus. Pre-operative forced duction test showed restriction in upward gaze. Orbital floor and inferior orbital rim accessed via lower lid skin crease incision. Orbital contents elevated. Pre-formed Synthes orbital floor reconstruction plate placed across the defect and secured to the inferior orbital rim with [#] [length] mm self-drilling screws. Posterior stop identified at [X] mm from orbital rim. Infraorbital nerve not visualized and protected throughout. Post-operative forced duction test showed normal range of motion with EOMI.

 

The patient was identified in the preoperative holding area. Risks, benefits, alternatives, and complications of the surgery were reviewed. Consent was verified. All questions were answered. The patient was escorted to the operating room and placed in supine position on the operating table. Anesthesia monitors were placed. The patient was placed under general anesthesia and intubated via a nasal endotracheal route without incident. The patient was then prepped and draped for surgery in the usual sterile fashion.

 

Attention was then drawn to the [right/left] orbit which was marked and grossly edematous. A forced duction test was performed showing a true restriction in upward gaze. Corneal shields with ophthalmic lubricant were then placed into the [right/left] eye. Next an incision was marked on the lower lid well below about 7 mm below the lower lid in a skin crease. 3 cc of 2% lidocaine with 1:100k epi was placed within the surgical site. The incision was made through skin and orbicularis oculi with a #15 blade, and a needle tip bovie was used to achieve adequate hemostasis. Blunt dissection was then carried down to the inferior orbital rim and once at the level of the periosteum, a sharp incision was made down to bone. A #9 periosteal elevator was used to dissect and expose the orbital floor and inferior orbital rim in a subperiosteal plane. The infraorbital nerve was not visualized and remained protected throughout the procedure. The large orbital floor fracture/defect was identified with orbital contents herniating into the maxillary sinus. The orbital contents were then gently and atraumatically elevated back into the orbit and native un-fractured and stable orbital bone was exposed on all sides of the fracture defect. The posterior stop measured to be [X] mm from the orbital rim. With all of the orbital tissues retracted superiorly, a pre-formed Synthes orbital floor reconstruction plate was measured, cut and tried in for size. Once the plate was well-adapted across the orbital floor defect, perched on solid bone, and all orbital contents were elevated superiorly, the reconstructive plate was secured to the inferior orbital rim utilizing [#] self-drilling [length] mm screws. The plate was noted to be well secured and stable. Next the corneal shield was removed and a forced duction test was performed with findings of a normal range of motion without restriction or impingement in any direction of gaze. The surgical site was then thoroughly irrigated with sterile saline and noted to be hemostatic and without debris. Next periosteum was closed over the plate with several simple interrupted 5-0 vicryl sutures in a buried nature. Next the skin was closed with a running 4-0 plain gut suture. Ophthalmic sterile saline was then used to irrigate the eye and a final forced duction test was completed showing extraocular movements intact.

 

This completed the surgical case. The patient was turned over to the anesthesia team, where she was extubated without complications and transferred to the PACU in stable condition.
